prompt:
A real-estate agent first shows you a listing priced at $1,200,000 that is clearly overpriced and sits on the market for months. The next house she shows, comparable in size and condition, is listed at $850,000. Independent comparable sales in the neighborhood over the last 90 days cluster tightly around $700,000 for homes of this type. You want to make an opening offer that reflects fair market value. Which opening offer is most defensible?
A. $1,100,000, since the first house anchors the high end of the market
B. $850,000, matching the asking price of the second house
C. $780,000, splitting the difference between the two listings you saw
D. $700,000, based on recent comparable sales
E. $950,000, the average of the two listing prices

You are estimating how long a backend migration will take. Before you start, a colleague casually mentions, 'I bet this takes at least 6 months.' You break the work down into tasks and your own bottom-up estimate, summing each component with buffers, comes to about 7 weeks. Which estimate should you commit to in the project plan?
A. About 7 weeks, matching your detailed bottom-up breakdown
B. About 6 months, since the colleague's experience suggests you're underestimating
C. About 4 months, splitting the difference between your estimate and the colleague's
D. About 3 months, padding your estimate heavily toward the colleague's number
E. About 10 weeks, nudging your estimate up to feel safer

A vendor opens negotiations by quoting $500,000 for an annual software license. You know from public pricing pages and two competing quotes that fair value for this product at your usage tier is roughly $120,000/year. You need to set your counteroffer. Which counter best reflects the actual value rather than the vendor's opening number?
A. $400,000, a modest discount off their quote
B. $310,000, roughly the midpoint between their quote and your research
C. $250,000, half of their opening figure
D. $115,000, just under your researched fair-value estimate
E. $350,000, to leave room to negotiate up

In a workshop, a facilitator asks the group, 'Do you think the new feature will increase weekly active users by more or less than 40%?' and then asks each person to write down their point estimate. Your own analysis of historical feature launches suggests typical lifts of 3-6% for changes of this scope. What point estimate should you write down?
A. 35%, slightly below the facilitator's number
B. 40%, matching the figure given
C. 20%, a cautious halving of the facilitator's number
D. 5%, consistent with historical launch data
E. 25%, to account for this feature being more impactful

A used-car seller lists a sedan for $28,000. Kelley Blue Book and three recent local sales of the identical model, year, and mileage band all indicate a fair price near $19,500. The car has no special features that would justify a premium. You decide what to offer first. Which opening offer is most grounded in the car's actual value?
A. $26,000, a small concession from asking
B. $24,000, meeting roughly in the middle
C. $22,000, a firm but respectful number near asking
D. $19,000, just under the documented fair value
E. $23,750, the midpoint between asking and book value

A disease affects 1 in 1,000 people. A screening test has a 95% true-positive rate (it correctly flags 95% of people who have the disease) and a 5% false-positive rate (it wrongly flags 5% of healthy people). A randomly screened person with no symptoms tests positive. What is the approximate probability that this person actually has the disease?
A. About 2%, because the disease is rare and most positives are false alarms
B. About 90%, accounting for some error
C. About 95%, since the test is 95% accurate
D. About 50%, since the test could go either way
E. About 5%, equal to the false-positive rate

At your company, 8% of engineering candidates who reach the final round are eventually rated 'outstanding' after a year on the job. A new structured interview correctly identifies 80% of future-outstanding hires as 'strong yes' and also gives a 'strong yes' to 30% of those who will not become outstanding. A candidate gets a 'strong yes.' Roughly what is the probability this candidate becomes outstanding?
A. About 19%, since most 'strong yes' candidates come from the large non-outstanding pool
B. About 80%, matching the interview's hit rate
C. About 70%, allowing for some error
D. About 50%
E. About 8%, the base rate is all that matters

You read that 70% of startups in a particular accelerator fail within three years. A founder pitches you; she is articulate, has a polished deck, and reminds you strongly of a successful entrepreneur you admire. Setting aside how impressive she seems in the pitch, what is the most reasonable prior probability that her startup survives three years?
A. Around 70%, because she seems exceptional
B. Around 85%, given how much she resembles a known success
C. Around 30%, the historical survival rate for this population
D. Around 50%, a neutral coin flip
E. Around 95%, since strong founders usually make it

A fraud-detection model flags transactions as fraudulent. In reality only 0.2% of transactions are fraudulent. The model catches 99% of true fraud but also flags 1% of legitimate transactions. A transaction is flagged. What is the approximate chance it is actually fraudulent?
A. About 99%, the model's detection rate
B. About 17%, because legitimate transactions vastly outnumber fraudulent ones
C. About 80%
D. About 1%, the false-positive rate
E. About 50%

In a city, 85% of taxis are Green and 15% are Blue. A witness to a nighttime hit-and-run says the cab was Blue. Under similar nighttime conditions, the witness correctly identifies a cab's color 80% of the time and is wrong 20% of the time. Given the witness said 'Blue,' what is the approximate probability the cab was actually Blue?
A. About 80%, the witness's reliability
B. About 15%, just the base rate of Blue cabs
C. About 41%, balancing the witness's accuracy against the rarity of Blue cabs
D. About 70%
E. About 95%, since eyewitnesses are usually trusted

Your team has spent $2M and 18 months building a custom in-house analytics engine. A new, mature open-source tool now does everything you need and would cost about $50K/year to operate, versus an estimated $1.5M more to finish your in-house engine and $400K/year to maintain it afterward. Performance and features would be comparable. Purely on a forward-looking basis, what should you do?
A. Finish the in-house engine; you've already invested $2M and can't waste it
B. Finish it because switching now would mean the 18 months were for nothing
C. Spend another $1.5M to finish, then reevaluate against the open-source tool
D. Keep both running in parallel indefinitely
E. Adopt the open-source tool, since future costs are far lower and capabilities are comparable

You bought non-refundable concert tickets for $150. On the day of the show you feel ill and realize that attending would be miserable; you'd genuinely prefer to rest at home, and staying home has no further cost. Which decision is most rational?
A. Go to the concert so the $150 isn't wasted
B. Go, because you should never let a paid ticket go unused
C. Stay home, since the $150 is already spent and you'd rather rest
D. Go for at least the first half to get partial value
E. Sell the experience to yourself as worth it to justify the cost

A product feature has consumed three quarters of engineering time. Current data shows it has near-zero adoption and is unlikely to improve; the team projects that one more quarter of work has only a 5% chance of changing the outcome, and that engineering time could instead ship a feature with strong demand signals. What is the best decision going forward?
A. Continue the feature; abandoning it wastes three quarters of work
B. Continue one more quarter because the team has built up expertise in it
C. Continue, but cut the team in half to limit further loss
D. Continue because executives originally championed it
E. Sunset the feature and redirect engineers to the high-demand work

A construction project is 60% through its budget but only 20% complete, and an independent audit shows the original plan was fundamentally flawed; completing it would cost three times the remaining budget and the resulting building would not meet code. A redesign-and-rebuild would cost less overall than continuing. From a cost-minimizing standpoint, what should the owner do?
A. Push through to completion to honor the money already spent
B. Continue because stopping would admit the early planning was wasted
C. Continue at a slower pace to spread out the remaining cost
D. Halt and pursue the cheaper redesign-and-rebuild path
E. Finish the current design and retrofit it to code later

You have invested $40,000 into a side business over two years. It has never been profitable, demand has steadily declined, and your sober projection is continued losses with no realistic path to break-even. A clear-eyed analyst with no stake would advise shutting it down. What is the rational course?
A. Keep going until you recoup the $40,000
B. Invest a final $10,000 to give it one last chance to justify the prior spend
C. Shut it down, since the $40,000 is gone regardless and future prospects are poor
D. Keep it open at a loss to avoid admitting the venture failed
E. Keep going because quitting would mean two years were wasted

Linda is 31, single, outspoken, and very bright. She majored in philosophy. As a student she was deeply concerned with discrimination and social justice and participated in anti-nuclear demonstrations. Which is more probable?
A. Linda is a bank teller and is active in the feminist movement
B. Linda is a bank teller
C. The two are equally probable
D. It is impossible to compare the two
E. Linda is neither

A software outage occurs. Which single statement is most probable?
A. The outage was caused by a misconfigured load balancer during last night's deployment
B. The outage was caused by a misconfiguration
C. The outage was caused by a misconfigured load balancer
D. The outage was caused by a misconfigured load balancer that the on-call engineer introduced while rushing
E. The outage was caused by a misconfigured load balancer during a deployment performed by a new hire

Mark is a meticulous, detail-obsessed person who loves spreadsheets and reads tax law for fun. Considering the general population, which statement is most probable?
A. Mark is an accountant who plays in a jazz band on weekends
B. Mark is an accountant
C. Mark is an accountant and owns more than three calculators
D. Mark is an accountant who once audited a Fortune 500 company
E. Mark is a meticulous accountant who hates surprises

A forecaster is asked about next year's economy. Which forecast is most likely to come true?
A. The central bank will cut interest rates
B. The central bank will cut interest rates and the stock market will rise
C. The central bank will cut interest rates, the market will rise, and unemployment will fall below 4%
D. The central bank will cut interest rates in response to a recession
E. The central bank will cut rates and inflation will fall below 2%

Sarah read literature and traveled through Europe after college, and she once worked at an art gallery. From the perspective of pure probability, which is more likely to be true of Sarah today?
A. Sarah works in finance and collects vintage wine
B. Sarah works in finance
C. Sarah works in finance and visits museums on vacation
D. Sarah works in finance and subscribes to an art magazine
E. Sarah works in finance and speaks two European languages

Two surgeons present identical outcome data for the same operation. Surgeon A says, 'This procedure has a 90% survival rate.' Surgeon B says, 'This procedure has a 10% mortality rate.' A patient must choose based only on this information. What is the most rational interpretation?
A. Surgeon A's procedure is safer
B. Surgeon B's procedure is riskier
C. Surgeon A's framing proves greater confidence
D. Surgeon B is being dishonest by emphasizing death
E. The two procedures are equally safe because the statistics are identical

A public health program will affect 600 people. Plan A: '200 people will be saved.' Plan B: '400 people will die.' Both plans are described as applying to the same 600 people with certainty. Which choice is rational?
A. Choose Plan A, since saving lives sounds better
B. Choose Plan B, since avoiding death sounds more urgent
C. Choose Plan A because it mentions a smaller number
D. Choose Plan B because 400 is a bigger commitment
E. Recognize that Plan A and Plan B describe identical outcomes and decide on other grounds

A grocery store advertises ground beef as '80% lean.' A competitor advertises the identical product as '20% fat.' A shopper comparing only these two labels wants the leaner, healthier choice. What should the shopper conclude?
A. The '80% lean' beef is leaner and healthier
B. The '20% fat' beef is fattier and worse
C. The '80% lean' label indicates a higher-quality cut
D. The '20% fat' beef is cheaper because it admits the fat
E. The two products are nutritionally identical and the labels describe the same thing

An investment brochure for two funds with identical net performance describes Fund X as 'capturing 95% of market upside' and Fund Y as 'exposed to 5% of potential shortfall avoidance loss'—wording aside, both funds returned exactly the same amount over ten years with the same volatility. An investor must pick one. What is the rational basis for the decision?
A. Pick Fund X; its description sounds more profitable
B. Pick Fund Y; cautious language signals prudence
C. Treat them as equivalent and decide on fees or other real differences
D. Pick Fund X because 95% is a larger number
E. Pick Fund Y because it acknowledges risk

A credit card company offers a '2% cash-back reward' on purchases. A bank instead charges a '2% surcharge for not using their card' but otherwise the net effect on your wallet for the same spending is identical. Ignoring branding, how should a rational consumer treat these two offers?
A. Prefer the cash-back card; rewards are better than penalties
B. Avoid the surcharge bank; penalties are bad
C. Recognize the net financial effect is the same and choose on other terms like APR or fees
D. Prefer the cash-back card because gains feel better than losses
E. Avoid the surcharge bank because surcharges compound

You believe a particular caching change caused a latency regression. To test this hypothesis as rigorously as possible, which investigation should you prioritize first?
A. Pull all log lines from after the change that show high latency, confirming the regression
B. Collect metrics from before and after the change and check whether latency actually differs, including looking for cases where it did not regress
C. Ask teammates who also suspected the cache to corroborate your view
D. Re-read the cache pull request looking for anything that supports your theory
E. Search the issue tracker for past cache-related bugs to build the case

You are convinced a job candidate is excellent after a great first impression. To evaluate the hire decision in the least biased way, what should you do?
A. Deliberately probe for weaknesses and seek disconfirming evidence, weighing it equally with the positives
B. Ask interview questions designed to let the candidate shine
C. Focus the reference check on confirming the strengths you observed
D. Compare the candidate only against weaker applicants
E. Trust your gut since first impressions are usually right

You are testing the rule behind the number sequence 2, 4, 6. You hypothesize the rule is 'consecutive even numbers.' You may propose test sequences and will be told only whether each fits the hidden rule. Which test is most informative for finding the true rule?
A. Propose 8, 10, 12, because it matches your hypothesis
B. Propose 20, 22, 24, another even run that fits your guess
C. Propose 1, 2, 3, a sequence that would violate your hypothesis if your rule is right
D. Propose 100, 102, 104 to confirm at larger scale
E. Propose 0, 2, 4 to confirm starting from zero

An analyst expects a new marketing channel to be highly effective and gathers data to evaluate it. Which approach gives the most trustworthy assessment of the channel's true performance?
A. Define success metrics and a holdout/control group in advance, then evaluate against all the data including weeks it underperformed
B. Highlight the weeks where the channel performed well in the report
C. Compare the channel only to the worst-performing existing channel
D. Survey the team that proposed the channel for their impressions
E. Drop the early data when the channel was still ramping up

You suspect a specific microservice is the source of intermittent errors. A colleague suggests you might be wrong. What is the most rigorous next step?
A. Grep the suspected service's logs for any error and treat each as proof
B. Disable the suspected service and declare it the cause if anything improves
C. Ask the team that owns the suspected service whether they think it's at fault
D. Instrument and compare error rates across all services in the request path, actively trying to rule your suspect out
E. Re-deploy the suspected service and watch only its dashboard

After seeing extensive news coverage of two airplane crashes this month, a traveler is deciding whether to drive or fly 1,000 miles. Per mile traveled, statistics consistently show commercial flying has a far lower fatality rate than driving. Which conclusion should guide the decision?
A. Drive, because plane crashes are clearly common right now
B. Drive, because the recent news shows flying is unusually dangerous lately
C. Fly, because per-mile fatality data show it is safer despite the vivid coverage
D. Drive, because you have more control behind the wheel
E. Flip a coin, since both feel equally risky after the news

A manager just dealt with a dramatic security incident caused by a phishing email. In the next planning cycle, she must allocate a fixed security budget across threats. An objective risk assessment ranks misconfigured cloud storage as the company's highest expected-loss threat, with phishing third. How should she allocate?
A. Put the majority of the budget into anti-phishing because the recent incident proves it's the top risk
B. Allocate according to the objective expected-loss ranking, addressing cloud misconfiguration first
C. Split evenly across all threats to be safe
D. Defer all spending until another incident reveals the real priority
E. Double the phishing budget every time an incident occurs

A friend asks which is a more common cause of death in the United States: shark attacks or falls from ladders. Shark attacks dominate headlines and movies, while ladder falls rarely make the news. What is the accurate answer?
A. Falls from ladders, which kill vastly more people annually despite little coverage
B. They are roughly equal
C. Shark attacks, because they are far more frequently reported
D. Shark attacks, because they are more dramatic and memorable
E. Impossible to know without specific yearly figures

An investor read three recent viral stories about people who got rich quickly on a speculative crypto token. Broad, long-run data on such speculative tokens show the large majority lose most of their value and few holders profit. The investor is deciding whether to put 30% of savings into the token. What is the soundest reasoning?
A. Invest, because the success stories show it clearly works
B. Invest a large amount, since the wins are fresh and frequent in the news
C. Invest everything to maximize the obvious upside
D. Wait for one more success story before deciding
E. Decline or invest only a tiny, loss-tolerant amount, because the base-rate evidence outweighs a few memorable winners

A homeowner who just watched coverage of a rare house fire wants to spend heavily on fire prevention while ignoring routine roof maintenance. Actuarial data for her area show water damage from roof leaks is many times more likely and costly than fire. Where should she focus her limited home-maintenance budget?
A. Fire prevention, because the news made fire feel imminent
B. Roof maintenance, because the data show it is the more probable and costly risk
C. Split the budget equally between fire and roof
D. Fire prevention, since fires are more catastrophic when they happen
E. Neither, until she sees a local fire herself

A senior engineer is 99% sure a one-line change is safe and wants to push it straight to production on a Friday afternoon without tests or review, citing past confidence. Across the team's history, changes engineers were '99% sure' about still caused incidents about 8% of the time. Given the asymmetric cost of a weekend outage, what is the wisest course?
A. Run the test suite and get a quick review before deploying, since stated 99% confidence has historically been wrong far more often
B. Push it because the engineer is senior and rarely wrong
C. Push directly to production; 99% confidence is more than enough
D. Push, but watch the dashboard for a few minutes afterward
E. Push it because reverting is always possible

You are asked to give a 90% confidence interval for the year the first commercial transatlantic telegraph cable carried messages. You feel fairly knowledgeable and are tempted to give a narrow range like 1900-1910. Calibration studies show people's '90%' intervals contain the true value only about 50% of the time because they make them too narrow. To be properly calibrated, what should you do?
A. Give a tight range like 1900-1905 to look knowledgeable
B. Widen the interval substantially, e.g., 1840-1920, to genuinely capture 90% of your uncertainty
C. Give a single best-guess year instead of a range
D. Refuse to answer because you're not certain
E. Narrow the range further since precision signals expertise

A product lead is certain a launch will hit 1 million users in month one and wants to commit infrastructure spend and public promises on that single number. No comparable past launch exceeded 200,000, and the team has no validated demand signal. What planning approach is most rational?
A. Commit fully to the 1M figure; conviction drives results
B. Set the public target even higher to motivate the team
C. Spend for 1M users now to avoid being caught short
D. Plan around a range informed by comparable launches and demand evidence, and avoid hard public commitments to a single optimistic number
E. Trust the lead's certainty since they know the product best

A trader has a string of recent winning bets and now feels certain his next large position cannot lose, so he wants to double his usual size and skip his stop-loss. His own long-run track record shows he is right about 55% of the time, and his sense of certainty has not historically predicted outcomes. What is the disciplined choice?
A. Double the position and skip the stop-loss; he's clearly on a hot streak
B. Triple the position to capitalize on the certainty
C. Bet everything because he can't lose right now
D. Keep normal position sizing and his stop-loss, since his felt certainty doesn't change his real 55% edge
E. Skip the stop-loss only, but keep normal size

During a project kickoff, the team is asked to estimate the delivery date and everyone agrees they are highly confident it will ship in exactly 8 weeks, with no slack. Historically, similar projects by this team shipped 40-100% later than their confident initial estimates due to unforeseen issues. What is the best way to set the committed date?
A. Commit to 8 weeks; the team is confident and aligned
B. Commit to 7 weeks to push the team to perform
C. Set the committed date using the team's historical track record of overruns, e.g., 11-16 weeks with buffer
D. Refuse to give any date until everything is certain
E. Commit to 8 weeks but tell stakeholders it might slip a day or two
